metadata.title: BEST LABOR DAY PARADE SPOKANE EVER MANY THOUSANDS IN Tin

pages.raw_text:
tLaDor'S day OF rest ana recreation
bfought out the union man and his
friends by the thousand today. Sew
eFal Thousand of the people who stand
for the tenets Of organised labor
participated in the parade, while
twice as many Of their wives, sweet
hearts. children and supporters lined
the sidewalks along the line Of march
for the big procession.


The parade started on time, as Ia
Dor has been in the habit Of doing
these many years. The start was
made at 10 o'clock sharp from First
avenue and Monroe street.


Promptly on the hour Presidents
Joyce and Quinn Of the two central
labor bodies clucked to their pranc-
ing black steeds. Hopes band blew
the first joyous notes Of hot tune
Just az Old Sol broke from cover
with his warmest sweltering rays Of
the day. Sergeant George Holiway
said something in an undertone
which started his blucoats into ac-
tion, the ofYlcers Of the two trades
assemblies swung into line. and the
big street show was started.


The line Of march was east on
First avenue to Post street, north to
Riverside avenue and east to Divis-
ion street, where the columns coun-
termarched and returned down Riv-
erside to Monroe street and south to
First avenue, where the union dis
banded to take the cars to the park.


First in line after the marshaIs.
police and band, were the officers
Of the central bodies, followed by
the speakers and members Of the
labor organ, the Spokane Record
The typographical union had the
place of honor in the parade as the
Oldest organization. The line was
made up as follows:


Typographical union.


Journeyman tailors.


Plasterers.


Building laborers.


Hod carriers.


Plumbers. steam and gas filters.


Occur d'Alene band.


Bricklayers.


Stone masons.


I. B. E. w.


Carpenters.


I. e. B.


Spokane band.


Cigarmakers.


Painters and decorators.


Carriages and automobiles.


Brewery workmen.


Prsssmen.


H0rseshoers.


Machinists.


Bakers and confectionery workers.
Hotel and restaurant employees.


Transfers.


Stationary engineers.


Hpokane Federal union.


Sheet metal workers.


B. r. c. Of A.


Blacksmiths and helpers.


Bartenders' league, teamsters, wag-
ons and floats.


E'hs largest turnout was by the
carpenters, who had 394 men in line.


The largest and best float in line
was undoubtedly that of the hotel
and restaurant employees. A man,
moth platform was surmounted with
reproduction of a restaurant and
dining room. prettily decorated and
with guests. cooks and walters at
